title: Launcher NativeAOT build needs vswhere.exe in PATH
desc: When publishing WKAppBot.Launcher with PublishAot=true, native linker fails with 'vswhere.exe is not recognized' error. Root cause: VS Build Tools' vswhere.exe path not in environment PATH. Quick fix: prepend Installer directory to PATH before dotnet publish.
steps:
SYMPTOM
  [error MSB3073] 'vswhere.exe' is not recognized as internal/external command
  Native AOT linker cannot locate MSVC link.exe because vswhere.exe PATH lookup fails.
  File location on Windows: C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Installer\vswhere.exe

FIX — prepend installer path in current shell
  export PATH="/c/Program Files (x86)/Microsoft Visual Studio/Installer:$PATH"
Then run the publish normally:
  "C:/Program Files/dotnet/dotnet" publish csharp/src/WKAppBot.Launcher/WKAppBot.Launcher.csproj -c Release
Only needed in Bash/Git-Bash shells where VS environment is not auto-loaded.
Developer PowerShell for VS has this path built-in.

PERMANENT FIX OPTIONS
  1. Add installer path to system PATH (System Properties → Environment Variables)
  2. Symlink: mklink /H C:\Windows\System32\vswhere.exe to the installer one (requires admin)
  3. Wrap build command in script that always prepends the path
  4. Use VS Developer Shell / Developer PowerShell which sets PATH automatically

WHY CORE DOESN'T NEED THIS
WKAppBot.CLI.csproj does NOT use PublishAot — regular dotnet publish works.
Only WKAppBot.Launcher.csproj has PublishAot=true (for fast startup ~100ms).
Thus Core builds succeed in plain Bash while Launcher needs PATH fix.

VERIFY
which vswhere.exe  # should print full path
Then retry publish. Look for 'Generating native code' in output.

RELATED
Published to bin/wkappbot.exe (~3.6MB single-file AOT).
If Launcher rebuild fails silently and you get old binary behavior, check output for the [MSB3073] vswhere error.
author: claude
